title: Cart Abandonment Correlation
spl:
index=web sourcetype="segment:track"
| transaction session_id maxspan=30m
| eval has_checkout=if(match(mvjoin(event,"|"),"Checkout"),1,0)
| eval has_order=if(match(mvjoin(event,"|"),"Order Completed"),1,0)
| where has_checkout=1 AND has_order=0
| stats count by checkout_error
| sort -count
description: Links payment errors, latency, and coupon issues to abandonment for revenue recovery.
value: Links payment errors, latency, and coupon issues to abandonment for revenue recovery.
grandmaExplanation: Links payment errors, latency, and coupon issues to abandonment for revenue recovery — so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: Privacy-compliant session stitching; A/B test fixes on top error codes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Web analytics, app logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="segment:track"`, `sourcetype="app:checkout"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Privacy-compliant session stitching; A/B test fixes on top error codes.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=web sourcetype="segment:track"
| transaction session_id maxspan=30m
| eval has_checkout=if(match(mvjoin(event,"|"),"Checkout"),1,0)
| eval has_order=if(match(mvjoin(event,"|"),"Order Completed"),1,0)
| where has_checkout=1 AND has_order=0
| stats count by checkout_error
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Cart Abandonment Correlation** — Links payment errors, latency, and coupon issues to abandonment for revenue recovery.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="segment:track"`, `sourcetype="app:checkout"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Web analytics, app logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: web; **sourcetype**: segment:track. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=web, sourcetype="segment:track". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Groups related events into transactions — prefer `maxspan`/`maxpause`/`maxevents` for bounded memory.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **has_checkout** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **has_order** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where has_checkout=1 AND has_order=0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by checkout_error** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Funnel (checkout steps), Bar chart (errors vs. abandon), Line chart (recovery campaigns).
visualization: Funnel (checkout steps), Bar chart (errors vs. abandon), Line chart (recovery campaigns).
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, backups, or batch jobs can drive metrics outside normal bands — correlate with change management windows.
